meta.title: The DMT Atlas
meta.tagline: A cartography of the collective experience — mapped from the reports.
meta.charter: This is a map of what people report and write about the DMT experience, synthesized from published books, peer-reviewed studies, and community archives. Every entry is cited. It is a cartography of a shared phenomenology and mythology — not a claim that these are literally real places or beings, and not a guide to obtaining, making, or taking any substance.
honesty: Map of reports, not metaphysics. Recurring entities/realms are what strangers consistently describe — that the descriptions converge is the interesting, documented fact; it is not proof of an external dimension.

The famous image is not the common one. 'Machine elves' are iconic (McKenna) but a minority percept (~2.9% of one report set; ~10% by one researcher's estimate). The single most-reported figure is a feminine/Goddess presence (~24%).

Numbers come only from named studies. Where a frequency is given, the study is named. 'More real than real' is felt near-universally but stated in those exact words by only ~0.6% (Lawrence 2022) — the feeling is pervasive, the literal phrase rare.
The journey order is a common scaffold, not a law. Every account differs; the waiting-room↔breakthrough line is largely dose-driven. Many people skip stages or land straight in the void.
It is not all love and safety. The benevolent majority (~78%) coexists with a fear/menace minority (~8% malicious; 41% felt fear at some point) and a 'you're not supposed to be here' current.

Some labels are cultural imports. 'Reptilian' and 'Grey' map onto pre-existing conspiracy/UFO scripts; researchers note the brain reliably foregrounds an agent, but 'the specific make-up of the agent is culturally determined' (Timmermann).

Source tiers: peer-reviewed surveys/field studies (Davis 2020, Lawrence 2022, Michael 2021, Timmermann 2018/2019) > clinical narrative (Strassman) & cross-substance scholarship (Shanon) > community lexicon (DMT-Nexus) & popular syntheses. McKenna is one vivid primary account, not a representative sample.

sources.author: Terence McKenna
sources.work: True Hallucinations / The Archaic Revival / lectures
sources.note: Coined 'self-transforming machine elves,' 'tykes,' 'jeweled self-dribbling basketballs,' 'syntax-driving light,' and the visible-language / Logos themes. A vivid individual account, not a representative sample.

sources.author: Rick Strassman, M.D.
sources.work: DMT: The Spirit Molecule
sources.note: Univ. of New Mexico clinical study, ~60 volunteers / ~400 doses (1990–1995). Source of 'they were waiting for me,' the high-tech nursery/lab realms, and the abduction-overlap observation. Clinical narrative, not a controlled frequency study.

sources.author: Davis, Clifton, Weaver, Hurwitz, Johnson & Griffiths (Johns Hopkins)
sources.work: Survey of entity encounter experiences occasioned by inhaled N,N-DMT
sources.note: Journal of Psychopharmacology. N=2,561 — the largest DMT entity survey. Descriptor %, benevolence, fear, reality-alteration and belief-change figures. Some figures here drawn from the abstract + reputable secondary summaries where the full text was paywalled; treat exact decimals as approximate.

sources.author: Lawrence, Carhart-Harris, Griffiths & Timmermann
sources.work: Phenomenology and content of the inhaled N,N-DMT experience
sources.note: Scientific Reports. Content analysis of 3,778 r/DMT reports — the source of the entity-phenotype and realm/geometry percentages. Samples people who self-select to post, so rates reflect that population.

sources.author: Michael, Luke & Robinson
sources.work: An Encounter With the Other: a thematic & content analysis of DMT experiences from a naturalistic field study
sources.note: Frontiers in Psychology. n=36 breakthrough-dose field study — beings 94%, other-space 100%, the role/appearance/demeanor breakdowns, the realm taxonomy (lattice/circus/machine worlds).

sources.author: Timmermann, Roseman, Williams, Carhart-Harris et al. (Imperial College)
sources.work: DMT Models the Near-Death Experience
sources.note: Frontiers in Psychology. Placebo-controlled comparison: DMT reproduces much of the Greyson NDE-scale profile; authors caution the contexts differ.

sources.author: Timmermann, Roseman, Schartner, Carhart-Harris et al. (Imperial College)
sources.work: Neural correlates of the DMT experience assessed with multivariate EEG
sources.note: Scientific Reports. N=13. Peak subjective effect 2–3 min after IV dose, elevated ~17 min; alpha-power drop and rising signal diversity.

sources.author: Michael, Luke et al.
sources.work: An Encounter With Death: comparative analysis of naturalistic DMT experiences and the NDE
sources.note: Frontiers in Psychology. Quantifies the DMT↔NDE overlap AND divergence — life-review and deceased relatives are rare in DMT (~1–6%) yet central to NDEs.

sources.author: David Luke
sources.work: Discarnate entities and dimethyltryptamine (DMT): psychopharmacology, phenomenology and ontology
sources.note: J. Society for Psychical Research. Foundational typology of recurrent entity types (elves, mantis 'surgeons,' jewel-encrusted reptilians) across independent reporters.

sources.author: Benny Shanon
sources.work: The Antipodes of the Mind: charting the phenomenology of the ayahuasca experience
sources.note: Oxford University Press. The most thorough systematic phenomenology of the closely related (same-molecule) ayahuasca state — serpents, royalty/palaces, temples, death/rebirth.

sources.author: Andrew R. Gallimore
sources.work: Alien Information Theory: psychedelic drug technologies and the cosmic game
sources.note: Reframes hyperspace as a real, orthogonal, higher-dimensional informational reality and the entities as genuine intelligences. An explicit ontological hypothesis, not established fact.

sources.author: Graham Hancock
sources.work: Supernatural: meetings with the ancient teachers of mankind
sources.note: Argues DMT/ayahuasca animal-human hybrids are continuous with cave-art therianthropes and folkloric faeries — the 'ancient teachers.' Speculative thesis.

sources.author: Peter Meyer
sources.work: 340 DMT Trip Reports (and 'Concerning the Nature of the DMT Entities')
sources.note: Canonical early archive: 226 of 340 reports (~66%) mention apparently independent entities; argues their cross-report consistency makes them 'intersubjectively verifiable.'

sources.author: Jennifer A. Lyke (Stockton University)
sources.work: DMT Entities: not everyone gets machine elves (analysis of 149 Erowid reports)
sources.note: Quantifies how the folk vocabulary maps to actual report frequencies — the corrective that 'machine elves' (~2.9%) is rarer than 'feminine' (~24%) or even 'robot/machine entity' (~6.7%).

sources.author: DMT-Nexus community
sources.work: The Hyperspace Lexicon
sources.note: Crowd-authored glossary that names the territory (Hyperspace, Breakthrough, Waiting Room, Chrysanthemum, Carrier Wave, the Void, Hyperslap, Machine Elves/Tykes). Folk-phenomenology; some entries are one contributor's idiosyncratic coinages.

sources.author: Erowid
sources.work: Experience Vaults (DMT)
sources.note: Large archive of first-person reports underlying many of the secondary analyses.

sources.author: Andrés Gómez Emilsson (Qualia Research Institute)
sources.work: The Hyperbolic Geometry of DMT Experiences
sources.note: A six-level model (Threshold, Chrysanthemum, Magic Eye, Waiting Room, Breakthrough, Amnesia) and the argument that the geometry is hyperbolic, which explains its un-rememberability. An interpretive theory, not established fact.

sources.author: David Jay Brown & Sara Phinn Huntley
sources.work: The Illustrated Field Guide to DMT Entities
sources.note: A 25-entity taxonomy synthesizing the popular literature; provides structured entity categories (qualitative, not frequency-measured).

sources.author: Jeremy Narby
sources.work: The Cosmic Serpent: DNA and the origins of knowledge
sources.note: Proposes the cosmic-serpent = DNA hypothesis after Asháninka fieldwork. Evocative but untestable and criticized for confusing enthusiasm with evidence — flagged as speculation.

sources.author: Carl G. Jung
sources.work: The Archetypes and the Collective Unconscious
sources.note: Supplies the standard naturalistic framing for why unrelated strangers report similar figures (Mother, Trickster, Shadow). An interpretive lens; inherited archetypes are not empirically established.

sources.author: Mircea Eliade
sources.work: Shamanism: archaic techniques of ecstasy
sources.note: The three-worlds / axis-mundi cosmology reached by ecstatic technique. Standard reference, but criticized (Kehoe, Hutton) for over-universalizing distinct traditions.

sources.author: Michael Harner
sources.work: The Way of the Shaman
sources.note: 'Core shamanism' tunnel-journey to a Lowerworld 'teeming with beings,' built on Conibo/Jívaro ayahuasca apprenticeship. Criticized for decontextualizing diverse traditions.

sources.author: Raymond Moody
sources.work: Life After Life
sources.note: Coined 'near-death experience' and defined its canonical elements (OBE, tunnel, light, deceased, life-review, the border). Foundational but anecdotal by design.

sources.author: Bruce Greyson, M.D.
sources.work: After / the Greyson NDE Scale
sources.note: Fifty years of NDE research; source of 'realer than real,' memory stability, and the scale DMT is measured against. Empirically cautious.

sources.author: John E. Mack, M.D.
sources.work: Abduction: human encounters with aliens
sources.note: Harvard psychiatrist who documented the abduction 'examination' script. Heavily criticized for hypnosis/suggestion methodology; presented alongside the skeptical counter-explanation.

sources.author: Susan A. Clancy
sources.work: Abducted: how people come to believe they were kidnapped by aliens
sources.note: The skeptical counterweight: sleep paralysis + cultural script + false-memory proneness + suggestive hypnosis explain abduction belief without aliens.

sources.author: Graham St John
sources.work: The Breakthrough Experience: DMT hyperspace and its liminal aesthetics
sources.note: Anthropology of Consciousness. Liminality framing; hyperspace as infinite cathedrals/machine-scapes/geometric abstraction; the 'Universal/Gaian Mind' perceived as God.

sources.author: Pablo Amaringo & Luis Eduardo Luna
sources.work: Ayahuasca Visions: the religious iconography of a Peruvian shaman
sources.note: Primary visual record of mestizo ayahuasca cosmology — anaconda and jaguar spirits, plant/forest spirits, the 'plant teacher' doctrine.

entities.name: Self-transforming machine elves
entities.appearance: Small, hyperactive, jeweled and fractal beings in constant shape-shifting transformation; McKenna's 'jeweled self-dribbling basketballs' and 'self-dribbling Fabergé eggs,' made 'not of matter' but of 'syntax-driving light.'
entities.behavior: Greet the visitor with exuberant delight ('we're so glad to see you'), crowd forward, leap in and out of the chest to reassure, and frantically sing impossible objects into existence — urging the visitor to do the same.
entities.communication: A visible language: 'it is sound, but you see it.' Objects condensed out of colored speech; a telepathic 'upload.'
entities.emotional_tone: Joyful, comic, frantic, overwhelming — bordering on McKenna's 'death by astonishment.'
entities.message_or_purpose: 'Behold — do what we are doing.' To teach that language/imagination can be externalized and beheld.
entities.frequency: Iconic but a MINORITY percept — only ~2.9% of one report set used the literal term (Lyke), and one researcher estimates ~10% see the specific beings (Timmermann). Its fame is McKenna's doing, not its rate.

sources.detail: Coined the term; 'making objects come into existence by singing them into existence'; 'use your voice to make an object.'
'Machine elves' appears as a specific description in only ~2.9% of analyzed reports — the iconic term is statistically rare.
Mythological beings (incl. machine elves) ≈ 8.4% of entity encounters.
'Not that many people in our study saw elves.'

entities.name: The Divine Feminine
entities.appearance: A huge, intensely bright feminine presence of light — maternal, nurturing, sometimes erotic; in ayahuasca personified as 'Mother Ayahuasca,' the sentient spirit of the vine. Often felt more than seen.
entities.behavior: Enfolds, reassures, heals; 'caresses away all pain and tears,' shows the visitor their wounds and holds them so the pain can dissolve.
entities.communication: Wordless transmission of love, knowing, and acceptance.
entities.emotional_tone: Overwhelming love, tenderness, awe, homecoming; sometimes stern correction.
entities.message_or_purpose: 'You are loved / you are home / you already know.' Healing of emotional and maternal wounds.
entities.frequency: The SINGLE MOST COMMON entity phenotype — ~24.2% of entity encounters (Lawrence 2022), echoed by Lyke's ~24% and near-universal in ayahuasca framing. The real 'face' of hyperspace, far more than the elves.

sources.detail: Feminine phenotype ≈ 24.2% — the most common entity type coded.
'Feminine' (Goddess/Mother) the single most predominant description (~24%, almost 1 in 4).
'Mother Goddess — a huge, bright, feminine Goddess... caring, loving'; the 'Machine Elves' Queen' revealed as the source behind all the patterns.
Feminine presence among the most common phenotypes; nurturing/healing role.

entities.name: Deities & god-like presences
entities.appearance: Vast god-like presences — a supreme being, the Godhead, archetypal cultural deities (Egyptian, Hindu, Mayan), or a formless 'Universal/Gaian Mind' perceived through the visitor's own framework.
entities.behavior: Radiate authority and cosmic knowledge; may take keen interest or remain transcendent and aloof; range from infinitely compassionate to judging.
entities.communication: Telepathic, archetypal, revelatory; direct knowing 'through presence.'
entities.message_or_purpose: Revelation of ultimate reality; disclosure of 'the nature of things'; sometimes moral reckoning.
entities.frequency: ≈ 17% of entity encounters (Lawrence 2022). The encounter frequently rewrites metaphysics — ~80% said it altered their fundamental conception of reality (Davis 2020).

sources.detail: Deities ≈ 17.0% of entity encounters.
~80% said the encounter altered their fundamental conception of reality; many former atheists came to believe in a higher power.
The 'Universal Mind / Gaian Mind' perceived or interpreted as God or any ultimate being from the visitor's framework.
'Gods & Goddesses' resembling cultural archetypal godheads; 'The Logos' as the Universal/Gaian Mind.

entities.name: The generic 'Other' — guides, spirits & helpers
entities.appearance: Often a sensed presence as much as a seen creature — sometimes humanoid, sometimes form-light. The catch-all gestalt the literature calls 'the Other.'
entities.behavior: Welcome, guide, help, watch, and present objects/scenes for the visitor to attend to; the visitor often feels recognized ('they knew me').
entities.communication: Predominantly intuitive/telepathic — 'not language, just a knowing, a complete knowing' (~39%).
entities.emotional_tone: Charming and inviting (~56%); love, trust, kindness, joy; familiarity and homecoming.
entities.message_or_purpose: Reassurance, love, guidance, and 'downloads' of insight (interconnection, 'let go,' 'it's all a cosmic game — just enjoy').
entities.frequency: The DEFAULT encounter. 'Guide' (43%), 'spirit' (39%), 'alien' (39%), 'helper' (34%) were the top descriptors (Davis 2020); 'otherly creatures' 72% in the field study (Michael 2021).

sources.detail: Top descriptors being/guide (43%) / spirit (39%) / alien (39%) / helper (34%); dominant emotions love, kindness, joy; >75% benevolent.
'Otherly creatures' 72%; charming/inviting 56%; helping/nurturing 53%; telepathic 'complete knowing' 39%.
'The function of the beings is to communicate, and what they communicate is information.'

entities.name: Alien & 'Grey' forms
entities.appearance: Classic sci-fi aliens — Greys with large black eyes, or silvery, towering, slender beings whose faces are 'jumbled, abstracted, and leaking colored light,' often in technological settings.
entities.behavior: Investigative and clinical — observe, examine, 'process,' or conduct experiments/probes; can be neutral-curious or unsettling.
entities.communication: Telepathic or 'technologically mediated'; sometimes speech as cartoon-like captions.
entities.emotional_tone: Clinical, uncanny; awe at the technological otherness; occasional fear.
entities.message_or_purpose: To study, examine, or convey that humanity is being observed; sometimes information downloads. The core of the DMT↔abduction overlap.
entities.frequency: 'Alien' was a top descriptor at ~39% (Davis 2020); the alien phenotype ~16.3% (Lawrence 2022). Strassman noted the resemblance to abduction reports is 'stunning.'

sources.detail: 'Alien' chosen by ~39% as a descriptor; senses primarily visual + telepathic.
Aliens ≈ 16.3% of entity phenotypes.
'The overlap of many DMT experiences with those reported by alien abductees is stunning.'
Documented the grey-being 'examination' script in non-drug abduction experiencers (method later heavily criticized).

entities.name: Mantis & insectoid beings
entities.appearance: Towering (often 6–9 ft) insect-proportioned beings — triangular heads, large black eyes, multi-jointed limbs; mantis, moth, or grasshopper-like.
entities.behavior: Clinical and surgeon-like — 'leaning over you doing some weird operation on your brain,' performing examinations or procedures, sometimes 'harvesting emotions.'
entities.communication: Telepathic and businesslike — can 'telepathically reassure everything will be alright' even mid-procedure.
entities.emotional_tone: Cool, detached; awe shaded with apprehension — not warm, but often not hostile.
entities.message_or_purpose: To examine, operate on, or 'fix' the visitor — the strongest single overlap with the abduction 'medical examination' script.
entities.frequency: Occasional but strikingly cross-reported — Strassman's and Luke's canonical 'baffling predictability' type; ~6–11% of the field study's creature categories.

sources.detail: 'Preying mantis alien brain surgeons' appearing 'with baffling predictability.'
Volunteers reported insectoid/mantis examiners; encounters resembled alien abduction.
Insectoid proportions ~11%; grey/mantis-like ~6%.
'Human-sized praying mantises... perform surgical/medical experiments; often cold-hearted.'

entities.name: Reptilian beings
entities.appearance: Reptilian/saurian humanoids and serpents, sometimes 'jewel-encrusted'; green scaled skin, snout-like faces, large oval eyes.
entities.behavior: Ambiguous to unsettling — often cold and observing, occasionally framed as malevolent; less interactive than elves or mantises.
entities.communication: Non-verbal, through presence and action; telepathic when engaged.
entities.emotional_tone: Neutral-curious to fearsome; a cold, alien quality.
entities.message_or_purpose: Often opaque — observation, intimidation, or testing more than clear teaching.
entities.frequency: Occasional and canonical (Strassman/Luke), but among the most culturally LOADED labels — likely shaped by conspiracy/UFO 'reptilian' mythology as much as by the raw percept.

sources.detail: 'Jewel-encrusted reptilian beings' as a recurring 'baffling predictability' type.
Volunteers reported reptiles/lizards among the recurring forms.
'Larger than humans; green scaly skin; variable intentions — sometimes nefarious, occasionally offering wisdom.'

entities.name: The Jester / Trickster / Clown
entities.appearance: Clowns, jesters and harlequins in medieval-jester garb; sometimes mechanical clowns or top-hatted 'circus ringleaders' with coats, tails and a baton.
entities.behavior: Playful, mischievous, performative — pull the visitor into a shared 'show,' flash information-dense cards/objects, direct attention; can tip from liberating fun into mockery or cruelty.
entities.communication: Telepathy plus visual metaphor — flashing 'hyperspace tarot cards' and objects that carry information.
entities.emotional_tone: Mischief, hilarity, occasional unease; humor that can liberate or humble.
entities.message_or_purpose: To play, to subvert the visitor's seriousness, and to deliver the 'it's all a game — just enjoy' message.
entities.frequency: Occasional — jesters ≈ 6.5% of phenotypes (Lawrence 2022); 'mischievous/jestful' demeanor ~14% (Michael 2021). A canonical Strassman category.

sources.detail: Jesters ≈ 6.5% of entity encounters.
Mischievous/jestful demeanor ~14%; the 'it's a game, come and play' message.
'Jesters... clowns,' 'Hyperspace Jesters/Harlequins' flashing cards, 'Circus Ringleaders,' 'the Imps' (playful surface, darker core).
'Clowns, jokers and jesters' a distinct recurring class.

entities.name: Guardians & gatekeepers — 'the bouncer'
entities.appearance: Often a sensed presence or imposing figure at a threshold / 'waiting room' rather than a fixed form — felt more than seen.
entities.behavior: Guard and gate access to deeper hyperspace — prepare, 'vet,' or block the visitor; people report being shown where they want to go but 'not allowed to enter,' or being 'banned' for disrespect.
entities.communication: Telepathic permission or denial; a strong felt 'rule' or boundary.
entities.emotional_tone: Threshold anxiety, being judged or appraised; occasional reassurance once 'cleared.'
entities.message_or_purpose: To control access — you must be ready or permitted. Marks hyperspace as a rule-governed place with permissions.
entities.frequency: A distinct, repeatedly reported role — ~11% 'guardian' in the field study (Michael 2021); prominent in community 'locked out of hyperspace' lore.

sources.detail: 'Guardian' role ~11% (4/36).
The 'Shut-out' — 'sometimes you aren't let into the club by the bouncers'; the waiting room populated with telepathic 'ushers.'
Gatekeepers who 'control entrance to spirit realms; test worthiness.'

entities.name: Impersonal geometric intelligences — 'the machine'
entities.appearance: Not personified — animated sacred-geometric forms, lattices and structures that rotate, breathe, collapse and reform: a 'living mathematical intelligence.' Also discrete robot/machine beings.
entities.behavior: Largely indifferent to the visitor; carry structure and meaning rather than relating; perform routine higher-dimensional 'competence' that looks miraculous from below.
entities.communication: Minimal — meaning is embedded in the structure itself, apprehended directly rather than transmitted.
entities.emotional_tone: Awe, the numinous, cosmic vastness; sublime or coldly alien rather than warm.
entities.message_or_purpose: To reveal the fundamental structure of reality/consciousness — often with no 'wants' at all; an intelligence that simply IS.
entities.frequency: Among the MOST consistent features of the state — 'sentient structures' ~25% (Michael 2021). Discrete 'robot/machine' entities ~6.7% — notably more common than literal 'machine elves' (Lyke).

sources.detail: Sentient structures (animated geometric forms) ~25%; hyperdimensional structures ~17% (mostly tesseracts).
'Robot/machine entity' ~6.7% — more common than literal 'machine elves' (~2.9%).
'Tapestries — incomprehensible beings of peculiar dimensional properties... largely indifferent.'

entities.name: Light beings & angels
entities.appearance: Beings made entirely of light, so radiant that facial features are washed out; winged/angelic guardians.
entities.behavior: Watch over, guard, uplift, initiate; protective and benevolent; in the lexicon 'Archangels' rule a hierarchy of light beings by their depth of access.
entities.communication: Radiance and benign presence; authoritative transmission.
entities.frequency: A recurring benevolent class; the dominant tone of DMT overall is benevolent (>75%, Davis 2020).

sources.detail: 'Light Beings... strong radiance, always benign'; 'Archangels' ruling the hierarchy of light beings.
'Angels — radiant light beings,' comforting.

entities.name: Dark entities & demons
entities.appearance: Malignant, hostile, or shadowy forms — 'The Tormentor,' a tall, domineering figure shrouded in darkness; 'Cosmic Horrors'; or coolly amoral 'stylish, sexy demons' beyond good and evil.
entities.behavior: Chase, intimidate, torment, or attract negative attention; the community advises NOT to study, dwell on, or seek them.
entities.communication: Threat and oppression conveyed directly to the felt sense.
entities.emotional_tone: Dread, oppression — the most fearsome disposition (though the rarest overall).
entities.message_or_purpose: Punishment or confrontation with one's fear; sometimes simple indifference framed by the visitor as 'evil.'
entities.frequency: A minority — only ~8% perceived entities as malicious vs ~78% benevolent (Davis 2020), though 41% felt fear at some point. The community frames love/forgiveness as the 'true protection.'

sources.detail: 'Dark Entities' (don't study or seek them); 'The Tormentor,' 'Cosmic Horrors'; Love & Forgiveness as protection.
~8% malicious vs ~78% benevolent; 41% reported fear during the encounter.
'Fearsome or menacing' the least common disposition (~8%); one report of 'hooded dark creatures' warning 'Do not go there!'

entities.name: Deceased loved ones & ancestors
entities.appearance: Recognizable deceased people; in the field study humans appeared only as the deceased (no living humans).
entities.behavior: Greet, reassure, or deliver a terse message ('There is nothing here for you, kid!') before the scene shifts.
entities.communication: Telepathic recognition and reassurance; short verbal-feeling messages.
entities.message_or_purpose: Reassurance about continuity; guidance or redirection.
entities.frequency: RARE on DMT (~2%) — and a key DISANALOGY with near-death experiences, where deceased-relative reunions are central. The difference is likely shaped by expectation and context.

sources.detail: Humans ~17% of encounters and ONLY as deceased persons.
'Deceased family' (~2%), 'deceased friends' (~1%), 'life-review' (~6%) are LEAST common in DMT but quintessential in NDEs.
Meeting deceased loved ones is one of the canonical NDE elements — the contrast that makes its DMT rarity notable.

entities.name: The Cosmic Serpent
entities.appearance: An enormous, often iridescent snake — sometimes paired 'twin' serpents or a coiled double-helix; felt as the indwelling 'mother' spirit of the ayahuasca vine.
entities.behavior: Encircles, swallows, or unwinds before the drinker; presented as a teacher and guardian rather than a threat once trust is established.
entities.communication: Wordless transmission of knowledge, songs (icaros), and bodily 'downloads.'
entities.emotional_tone: Primordial awe and fear giving way to reverence and trust.
entities.message_or_purpose: Source of healing knowledge and the 'origin of knowledge' — in Narby's reading, a possible symbol of DNA itself.
entities.frequency: The single most common ANIMAL motif across ayahuasca/DMT visions, and serpents top cross-cultural creation myths — invoked as Jungian archetype and, more speculatively, as Narby's DNA symbol.

sources.detail: Narby's own vision of two intertwined snakes; a cross-cultural twin/creator-serpent survey across unrelated cultures.
Serpents among the most frequently reported beings across his large ayahuasca corpus.
The anaconda as a principal spirit ally in mestizo ayahuasca iconography.

entities.name: The Logos — the Voice
entities.appearance: Disembodied — a voice rather than a seen form; distinct from the seen elves. McKenna distinguished the heard Logos from the visible elf-language.
entities.behavior: Speaks 'through' the visitor, steady and articulate; dispenses teaching and prophecy.
entities.communication: An interior auditory voice — 'steady, unhesitating, and articulate' — that works 'directly on the language centers.'
entities.message_or_purpose: To inform; the planetary/Gaian mind made audible; a guiding higher intelligence.
entities.frequency: Central to McKenna's framing; reframed by Gallimore as 'the code' underlying reality.

sources.detail: 'Speaking through me was the voice of another, a voice that was steady, unhesitating, and articulate.'
'At the ground of our reality there is a code running' — the informational substrate the entities reveal.

realms.name: Hyperspace
realms.description: The community's central name for the place reached after breakthrough — 'any place and time imaginable,' a 'Wonderland meets Alien Spacecraft' of kaleidoscopic halls, impossible geometry and folding rooms; a 'nexus of dimensions' inhabited by entities and felt as fully, autonomously real.
realms.what_happens_there: Entity contact and the 'performance' — objects sung into being, visible language, downloads. 100% of one field-study sample emerged into a qualitatively other space.

sources.detail: 'Hyperspace' = the place after breakthrough; geometric explosions of color, sound, emotion, information.
An alternate/higher dimension reported in ~25.2% of experiences (hyperspace specifically ~17.1%).
100% (36/36) emerged into a qualitatively different space/reality.
DMT as the 'doorway to hyperspace' — 'another place,' not a state of mind.

realms.name: The Waiting Room
realms.description: A liminal, architectural threshold space reached just after onset and before (or at the edge of) full breakthrough — an enclosed, impossibly geometric chamber compared to a doctor's waiting room, a games lobby 'outside of the game,' or a Day-Glo Victorian hall with living walls. The visitor often feels expected.
realms.what_happens_there: Immersion becomes total (opening the eyes no longer changes the scene); entities become autonomous and able to interact, often turning to welcome the visitor ('welcome back, where have you been?'). From here one breaks through deeper or returns.

sources.detail: Volunteers landed in 'high-tech nurseries' and 'alien laboratories' where beings seemed to be waiting.
'A holographic virtual landscape... analogous to a games lobby, a place outside of the game,' populated with telepathic 'ushers.'
Room-like spaces in 15.4% of reports; the 'waiting room' specifically in only ~2.8% — robust as a category, but more prominent in lore than in raw frequency.

realms.name: The Domed Cathedral
realms.description: A vast, often domed or vaulted interior of impossible scale — cathedral, temple, opera house, or palace. McKenna's canonical breakthrough scene: 'tastefully soffited indirect lighting, and the crawling geometric hallucinations along the domed walls.'
realms.what_happens_there: The stage for entity contact and the 'performance,' with an atmosphere of sacredness, grandeur, and ceremony — the sense of having arrived somewhere built for a purpose.

sources.detail: 'the tastefully soffited indirect lighting, and the crawling geometric hallucinations along the domed walls.'
Hyperspace 'contains massive or even infinitely large cathedrals, machine-scapes or abstract spaces made of geometric patterns.'
Cross-substance ayahuasca phenomenology repeatedly yields celestial and temple-like architecture.

realms.name: The Throne Room
realms.description: A reported sub-realm in which the visitor is brought before a single central, elevated, sovereign presence — God/Goddess, King, Queen, or Cosmic Mother. The WEAKEST-attested location: not a formal lexicon entry, but inferred from deity encounters and cross-cultural 'palaces and royalty.'
realms.what_happens_there: An audience or reckoning — the being is experienced as ancient, wise, and authoritative, delivering a teaching, judgment, or reassurance.

sources.detail: Deities/divine beings in 17.0% of encounters (the population from which 'throne' scenes arise).
Ayahuasca drinkers from disparate cultures recurrently see 'palaces and royalty.'
The 'Universal/Gaian Mind' perceived as God or an ultimate being.

realms.name: The Void
realms.description: The inverse of the content-rich realms: a dissolution into undifferentiated awareness with little or no imagery — 'a state, or place of nothingness, without time, matter or significance.' White, golden, or black.
realms.what_happens_there: Ego-dissolution and death-rehearsal — loss of the inner narrative, body-ownership, and subject-object distinction; merging with a featureless ground of being or pure light.

sources.detail: The Void: 'a state, or place of nothingness, without time, matter or significance. A state of non-existence.'
A void (white/golden/black) in ~6.2% of reports.
'The Void' and 'Bright Light(s)' among canonical NDE themes shared with DMT.

realms.name: The Workshop / Factory / Market
realms.description: Industrial, fabricative realms experienced as the 'innards of a machine' — conveyor belts, assembly lines, workshops where objects, language, or 'memes' are manufactured and traded. McKenna nicknamed the elves 'meme traders.'
realms.what_happens_there: Beings fabricate impossible objects (self-assembling Fabergé-like things), show or hand them over, and appear to be running some vast process — factory and marketplace of forms.

sources.detail: 'Mechanical conveyor belts of psychedelic energy' within the 'innards of a machine'; technological objects in ~22%.
The entities as 'meme traders' and 'art collectors' crafting intricate, self-assembling objects.

realms.name: Geometric Palaces & the Lattice
realms.description: Realms made entirely of structured geometry — jeweled, crystalline, or lattice/grid architecture experienced as the 'blueprint' or scaffolding of reality ('an empty blueprint of the universe... simple grid... infinite').
realms.what_happens_there: The visitor perceives an underlying geometric order to existence; structures tessellate, fractalate, rotate and fold; hyper-dimensional objects appear.

sources.detail: Gridwork/lattice 'nebulous worlds' ~36%; geometric objects 44% (hyperdimensional cubes that 'fractalate').
Hyperspace as 'abstract spaces made of geometric patterns,' infinitely large geometric architecture.

realms.name: The Circus / Playroom
realms.description: A playful, whimsical, often childlike or cartoonish realm — circuses, carnivals, nurseries full of toys and capering entities, reality 'folding and unfolding.'
realms.what_happens_there: Trickster/jester entities cavort and invite the visitor to 'come and play'; the mood is mischievous and comedic — closely tied to the 'cosmic joke' and 'it's all a game' messages.

sources.detail: 'Children's worlds' ~8%: a 'playroom' with 'toys and entities and kids'; a world 'like a circus.'
Jesters/clowns as the entity phenotype in ~6.5% of encounters.

realms.name: Tunnels & Corridors
realms.description: A transit structure rather than a destination — geometric tunnels or corridors that dominate the field during the rush, conveying fast or accelerating movement toward 'somewhere else.'
realms.what_happens_there: Translocation — the felt sense of being pulled or launched through a passage at speed, on the way to the waiting room or breakthrough.

sources.detail: A tunnel-type structure in ~10.3% of reports.
'Tunnel-like structures' among canonical NDE themes shared with DMT.

realms.name: The Membrane / Veil
realms.description: The boundary surface one passes through to break through — often the chrysanthemum itself, 'a permeable membrane of initial hyperspace that either must be passed through or will be a wall locking you out.'
realms.what_happens_there: The pivotal transition — a felt rupture or push 'through' a surface, after which the perceptual world changes wholesale. McKenna: 'I was pushed from behind and I fell through the chrysanthemum into another place.'

sources.detail: 22% reported the distinct sensation of breaking through a veil/membrane; 58% a distinct arrival into a different environment.
'pushed from behind and I fell through the chrysanthemum into another place.'
The chrysanthemum/waiting-room boundary as 'a permeable membrane... pass through or be walled out.'

realms.name: The In-Between
realms.description: 'The world where you arrive before you break into Hyperspace — a world where you are on your own, already left this plane, but not yet reached the other.'
realms.what_happens_there: A solitary transitional zone between consensus reality and hyperspace — alone, in transit.

sources.detail: Verbatim: arrive before breaking into Hyperspace, on your own, left this plane but not yet reached the other.

realms.name: The Deeper Realms
realms.description: Community-acknowledged realms BEYOND the initial hyperspace entry — often culturally themed (Egyptian, Mayan, Hindu) and framed as 'not Hyperspace, but the realms of those gods'; a gradient, not strictly numbered levels.
realms.what_happens_there: Encounters with culture-specific deities and worlds. Notably, the community reports dark entities MORE in these 'other dimensional spaces' than in hyperspace proper — one offered marker of 'genuine' hyperspace is the absence of dark entities.

sources.detail: Culturally-themed realms = 'realms of those gods'; Dreamtime realms; 'the Astral'; a gradient, not discrete levels; dark entities more frequent here.
Cross-cultural visions of distinct 'higher' realms in the closely related ayahuasca state.

geometries.name: The Chrysanthemum
geometries.description: The classic onset mandala — a gigantic, often spinning, kaleidoscopic fractal flower with a domelike appearance that turns into a doorway, welcoming or blocking access to breakthrough. McKenna: 'a flower, like a chrysanthemum in orange and yellow that was sort of spinning, spinning.'

sources.detail: 'A gigantic, often spinning, kaleidoscopic fractal flower... that turns into a doorway, either welcoming or blocking access to breakthrough Hyperspace.'
'a chrysanthemum in orange and yellow that was sort of spinning, spinning.'
A textured surface of 'intricate symmetrical relationships, bright colors, shifting edges and shimmering pulsing superposition patterns.'

geometries.name: Fractal lattices & jeweled tilings
geometries.description: Nested, self-similar fractal patterning and tessellating tilings saturating every surface — jeweled, crystalline, gridwork lattices that fractalate and split. QRI argues these are hyperbolic tilings of negative curvature.

sources.detail: 'Fractals, geometric shapes, and patterns' (incl. kaleidoscopes, mandalas) in ~32.6% of reports.
Surfaces 'saturated with nested 2-dimensional symmetry groups,' composed of hyperbolic tilings with negative curvature.
Jeweled, self-transforming, language-like geometry.

geometries.name: The Carrier Wave
geometries.description: An auditory-vibrational phenomenon at onset — a high-pitched ringing tone, droning frequency, or buzzing 'carrier wave' that resonates 'in every cell of the body,' often paired with a perceptual 'wobble' as the visual field destabilizes. McKenna heard 'a sound like the crumpling of a plastic bread wrapper... and a tone. A hummmm.'

sources.detail: 'After a launch, travelers often hear a ringing tone or vibration somewhat resembling a carrier wave... resonating in every cell of the body.'
'a sound like the crumpling of a plastic bread wrapper... and a tone. A hummmmmm.'
An auditory ringing-type sound reported in ~15.4% of reports.

geometries.name: Hyper-dimensional objects
geometries.description: Impossible solids that exceed 3D — rotating hypercubes (tesseracts), self-assembling jeweled objects ('Fabergé eggs from Mars'), cubes that 'slip' between dimensions and fractalate. QRI ties their un-rememberability to geometry that cannot be embedded in ordinary space.

sources.detail: Hyperdimensional structures in 6 participants (5 cubes); a cube that 'slipped... fractalated... multiple dimensions.'
Entities sang into existence 'objects which looked like Fabergé eggs from Mars.'
Memories bias toward forms representable in ordinary 3D space; true hyperbolic objects resist recall.

geometries.name: Living language / visible sound
geometries.description: Synesthetic geometry in which heard sound becomes seen object — 'things heard become things seen.' McKenna's elves 'spoke in some kind of colored language which condensed into rotating machines'; survey participants saw hieroglyphs and ancient scripts.

sources.detail: Elves 'making objects come into existence by singing them into existence'; 'use your voice to make an object.'
Symbolic objects in ~17%: 'Egyptian hieroglyphical writings,' ancient language/hieroglyphics.

geometries.name: Mandalas & symmetry fields
geometries.description: Radially symmetric, concentric closed-eye geometric forms — the chrysanthemum is the canonical instance, but mandalic patterning recurs throughout the early phases with intense color and complex overlay.

sources.detail: Mandalic, symmetry-rich closed-eye visuals at the chrysanthemum level; connected to all 17 wallpaper symmetry groups.
Mandalas included within the ~32.6% reporting fractals/geometry/patterns.

geometries.name: Static Pattern Overlay & filigree
geometries.description: Uniform or random surfaces taking on a clearly visible pattern — arabesques, hieroglyphs, plants, animals and faces — plus spirals, ornamentation and filigree that decorate hyperspace 'as if being designed in real-time.' (Several of the finer lexicon names here are idiosyncratic community coinages.)

sources.detail: 'Static Pattern Overlay' — surfaces taking on arabesques/hieroglyphs/faces; 'Schnörkel' filigree decorating hyperspace in real-time.

phases.name: Inhalation / Onset
phases.description: Within ~3–30 seconds, before clear sensory change, a shift in the 'ambiance' — a sudden sharpening and brightening of the senses, things acquiring 'great significance,' often with a body vibration and the onset of the carrier-wave ringing.

sources.detail: Threshold (3–30s): change in ambiance, 'sudden sharpening of all the senses.'
Peak subjective effect 2–3 min after IV dose; alpha-power drop tracks intensity.

phases.name: The Chrysanthemum
phases.description: Closed-eye mandalic geometry blooms — the spinning orange/yellow chrysanthemum, dense fractal overlay, intense color, an organic hum. A threshold marker and doorway: break through it to go deeper, or stay at its surface.

sources.detail: The chrysanthemum as the doorway welcoming or blocking access to breakthrough.
The spinning orange-and-yellow chrysanthemum at the threshold.

phases.name: The Rush
phases.description: An overwhelming acceleration — fast movement along geometric tunnels, an ascending or intensifying sound (the carrier wave crescendoing) that 'suddenly switches to a new environment.' Often felt as being launched at great speed.

sources.detail: Tunnel-type structures ~10.3%; ascending auditory ringing ~15.4%.
'Freight train' rush descriptions across reports.

phases.name: Through the Membrane
phases.description: The pivotal rupture — a felt push 'through' a surface (often the chrysanthemum-as-door), after which the perceptual world changes wholesale. ~22% explicitly report breaking through a veil or membrane.

sources.detail: 22% reported breaking through a veil/membrane.
'pushed from behind and I fell through the chrysanthemum into another place.'

phases.name: Breakthrough
phases.description: Full immersion into hyperspace — 'so immersive you won't be questioning whether you made it.' The structure of perception and consciousness shifts; geometry becomes 'non-simply connected,' endlessly morphing; the realm feels hyper-real. For some this opens into the Void instead of a furnished scene.

sources.detail: Breakthrough: 'a singularity beyond life or death... endlessly morphing and fractalizing detail.'
Topological bifurcations; space 'non-simply connected'; entities feel 'transpersonal.'

phases.name: Arrival — The Waiting Room
phases.description: The visitor finds themselves in a structured location (waiting room / reception / nursery). Immersion is total; opening the eyes no longer changes the scene; entities become present, autonomous, and seemingly expectant.

sources.detail: Arrival in a 'room' where entities were 'waiting' and 'excited to see' the volunteer.
The waiting room as a 'games lobby' populated with telepathic 'ushers.'

phases.name: Entity Contact
phases.description: Encounter with seemingly autonomous, sentient beings — guides, helpers, spirits, deities, aliens, jesters, machine elves. Reported by the large majority at breakthrough; the beings appear curious, welcoming, and aware of the visitor.

sources.detail: 34/36 (94%) encountered sentient beings; 56% charming/inviting, 53% helping/nurturing.
Entity encounters in 45.5% of the broad report set.
Most common labels being/guide/spirit/alien/helper; encounters evoke 'profound love, surprise, and trust.'

phases.name: The Download / Lesson
phases.description: Information transfer — usually telepathic/intuitive ('not language, just a knowing, a complete knowing'), or via visible language, gesture, or presented objects. Messages cluster on love, 'let go,' 'it's all a game,' and the nature of reality.

sources.detail: Telepathic 'complete knowing' (~39%); messages of love, 'let go,' 'it's a game... come and play.'
The visible Logos — being shown and taught.
Volunteers reported telepathic 'downloads' from the beings.

phases.name: The Peak / Throne / Apex
phases.description: The deepest reach for some — an audience with a central sovereign/divine presence, or conversely a peak of ego-dissolution, mystical union, or the Void. Strongly tied to mystical-experience and death-acceptance content. Highly variable; not everyone reaches it.

sources.detail: Mystical-type experiences in ~71.4%; deities in ~17% of encounters.
The 'Universal/Gaian Mind' perceived as God or an ultimate being.

phases.name: The Return
phases.description: The short-acting molecule wears off (minutes for inhaled DMT); the visitor is pulled back through the layers — geometry recedes, the waiting room dissolves — and rematerializes into ordinary space, often abruptly. Entities may bid farewell ('come back').

sources.detail: On return, only forms embeddable in ordinary 3D space are easily 'brought back.'
Subjective intensity elevated ~17 min, then declines.

phases.name: Afterglow / Integration
phases.description: Post-experience wonder, emotional openness, and a struggle to verbalize — often a frustrating amnesia, the sense of having known something profound that can't be recalled. Enduring worldview shifts (even atheists coming to believe the entity/God is real) are common.

sources.detail: Lasting changes in worldview; many former atheists came to believe in God; 72% believed the entity continued to exist afterward.
The 'amnesia' problem: hyperbolic content resists ordinary recall.

themes.name: More real than real
themes.description: The realm and its beings feel more vivid, solid, and authentic than waking life. Strassman: the entities 'feel more real than real, but their ontological foundation cannot be determined.' Felt near-universally — yet stated in those exact words by only ~0.6% (Lawrence 2022); the FEELING is pervasive, the literal phrase rare.

sources.detail: Entities 'feel more real than real, but their ontological foundation cannot be determined.'
Only ~0.6% used the literal phrase, though hyper-vividness is pervasive.
A hyper-real quality reported by survey respondents.

themes.name: They were expecting me
themes.description: A pervasive sense of being anticipated, recognized, and welcomed — entities 'excited to see' the visitor, saying 'welcome back, where have you been?' McKenna's elves 'assured me that they loved me and told me not to be amazed.'

sources.detail: Volunteers reached a 'room' where entities were 'waiting' and 'excited to see' them.
'they assured me that they loved me and told me not to give way to astonishment.'

themes.name: You're not supposed to be here
themes.description: A counter-current to the welcome — a sense of intruding on a domain not meant for the living, of having 'broken in.' Some entities warn the visitor off; the lexicon casts breakthrough as 'a singularity beyond life or death' and the waiting room as a threshold for the recently deceased.

sources.detail: 'Hooded dark creatures' warning 'Do not go there!'; 8% found entities fearsome/menacing.
Breakthrough 'a singularity beyond life or death'; the waiting room a portal point for the recently deceased.

themes.name: Remember — you already knew this
themes.description: Overwhelming déjà-vu and a sense of returning home / remembering something forgotten — 'intense familiarity combined with absolute amazement,' entities like 'my sisters.' The punchline of the cosmic joke: you went to all this effort to find out what you already knew.

sources.detail: 47% felt 'one with' the beings; entities 'like my sisters'; 'we knew each other so well.'
The familiarity ties to the un-rememberability — 'if only you could remember.'

themes.name: The cosmic joke
themes.description: An atmosphere of intense hilarity and the punchline that 'it's all a game' — visitors feel let in on a cosmic joke. The confrontation with the mind's inability to make sense of existence resolves into laughter; the jester embodies it.

sources.detail: The 'cosmic giggle' and the comic register of the elves.
Beings: 'it's a game, it's all a game,' 'come and play.'

themes.name: Overwhelming love & interconnection
themes.description: Profound, often unconditional love radiating from the beings and the realm, with feelings of unity and oneness. The most commonly transmitted message is love — for self and others.

sources.detail: Encounters 'frequently evoke profound love, surprise, and trust'; >75% benevolent.
The most common message: 'love for others and self.'

themes.name: Death-rehearsal & ego dissolution
themes.description: A confrontation with dying and the dissolution of the self — loss of inner narrative, body-ownership, and subject-object distinction. DMT reproduces much of the NDE profile, and many report acceptance / loss of fear of death afterward.

sources.detail: DMT reproduces much of the Greyson NDE-scale profile (translocation, bright light, sense of dying, the void, disembodiment).
Ego-dissolution items ~6.9%; 25% of death-theme experiences endorsed acceptance / removal of fear of death.

themes.name: Information, language & the Logos
themes.description: The experience is saturated with meaning conveyed as information, symbol, and language — McKenna's thesis that the entities are 'made of language' and that one can 'use your voice to make an object.' Telepathic knowing, hieroglyphs, and downloads recur.

sources.detail: Entities 'made not of matter, but of language,' who 'sang syntax into being.'
Symbolic objects/hieroglyphics ~17%; telepathic 'complete knowing.'

themes.name: Being shown a teaching or object
themes.description: Entities act as 'presenter' — proffering an object, tablet, or self-assembling artifact, or directing attention to something to be learned. McKenna's elves 'proffered strange little tablets'; ~47% of field-study entities were 'showing or communing.'

sources.detail: 47% 'showing or communing'; entities as 'presenter' of objects (10/36).
'insect elf machines proffering strange little tablets'; Fabergé-egg objects.

themes.name: Benevolence over malevolence
themes.description: Entities are far more often experienced as benevolent and loving than hostile — but the realm is not uniformly safe: a fear/menace minority and the 'transgression' current coexist with the love.

sources.detail: Benevolent ~78%; malicious/judgmental <15%; yet 41% felt fear at some point.
Charming/inviting 56% vs fearsome/menacing only 8%.

themes.name: Ontological shock & belief change
themes.description: The encounter commonly rewrites the visitor's conception of reality and of whether the entity was 'real' — a durable shift, including away from atheism toward belief in a higher power.

sources.detail: ~80% said it altered their fundamental conception of reality; ~72% believed the entity continued to exist; >50% of former atheists changed.

themes.name: Telepathy is the norm
themes.description: Communication with the 'Other' is characteristically wordless — telepathy, gesture, sung/objectified language, or direct 'knowing' — a consistent formal feature across DMT, ayahuasca, NDE, and abduction reports.

sources.detail: Majority reported telepathic/gestural communication; senses primarily visual + extrasensory.
Telepathic/intuitive 'complete knowing' the dominant mode (~39%).

themes.name: Convergence across traditions
themes.description: The DMT universe rhymes with humanity's wider visionary record — ayahuasca (the SAME molecule), the shamanic three-worlds/tunnel-journey, the NDE (tunnel, light, hyper-reality), and the alien-abduction 'examination.' The convergence is real at the level of phenomenology and (for DMT/ayahuasca) chemistry — but it does NOT by itself prove a shared external reality, and the parallels are partial: life-review and deceased relatives are central to NDEs yet rare in DMT.

sources.detail: DMT reproduces much of the Greyson NDE profile — but the authors caution the contexts differ.
Quantifies the overlap AND the disanalogy: life-review/deceased rare in DMT.
The serpent as a near-universal motif across ayahuasca visions and world creation myths (the DNA reading is speculative).
The shamanic three-worlds / tunnel-journey to an inhabited realm prefigures the DMT report.
'Core shamanism': a drum-induced tunnel-journey to a Lowerworld 'teeming with beings.'
The collective unconscious as the standard naturalistic framing for why strangers report similar figures.
The DMT/ayahuasca animal-human hybrids as continuous with cave-art therianthropes and folkloric faeries — the 'ancient teachers.'
NDEs rated 'realer than real' with memories that stay vivid for decades — the same hyper-reality DMT reports.
The alien-abduction 'examination by greys' script overlaps the DMT mantis/grey encounter.
The skeptical counter-explanation: sleep paralysis + cultural script + false memory can produce abduction belief without aliens — a reminder that convergence isn't proof.

data_points.claim: Largest survey of DMT entity encounters
data_points.detail: Johns Hopkins online survey of the single most memorable inhaled-DMT entity encounter.
Most-selected labels for the entity.
Dominant emotions love, kindness, joy; ~70% rated the entity 'sacred.'

data_points.claim: Felt fear at some point during the encounter
data_points.detail: Despite the benevolent majority — the realm is not uniformly safe.

data_points.claim: Said the encounter altered their fundamental conception of reality
data_points.detail: And ~72% believed the entity continued to exist after the experience.

data_points.claim: Prior atheists who no longer identified as atheist afterward
data_points.detail: A durable shift toward belief in a higher power.
Lawrence et al., Scientific Reports.

data_points.claim: Entity encounters across all inhaled-DMT reports
data_points.detail: Of 3,778 experiences.

data_points.claim: Most common entity phenotype — the feminine/Goddess
data_points.detail: More common than any other type — and far more than 'machine elves.'
Second most common entity phenotype.
Entity phenotype.
Entity phenotype.
The 'machine elf' category is a minority — its fame outruns its rate.
Entity phenotype.
Realm content.
Robust as a category, far rarer by the exact label.
White, golden, or black.
Realm/transit content.
Including kaleidoscopes and mandalas.
Buzzing/humming/high-pitched tone.

data_points.claim: Used the literal phrase 'more real than everyday reality'
data_points.detail: The FEELING is near-universal; the exact wording is rare — don't over-read the number.
Of the report set.

data_points.claim: Naturalistic field study — encountered sentient beings
data_points.detail: Breakthrough-dose inhaled DMT (mean 54.5 mg).

data_points.claim: Field study — emerged into a qualitatively different space
data_points.detail: Every participant.

data_points.claim: Field study — broke through a veil/membrane
data_points.detail: A distinct arrival into a different environment in 58%.

data_points.claim: Field study — entity demeanor charming/inviting vs fearsome
data_points.detail: Benevolence dominates, menace is the minority.
The iconic term is statistically rare; 'robot/machine entity' (~6.7%) is actually more common.

data_points.claim: Estimated share who see the specific McKenna-style beings
data_points.detail: Timmermann's estimate; the recurrence is 'overstated' by McKenna's fame.

data_points.stat_or_value: significant overlap on the Greyson NDE scale
data_points.detail: Placebo-controlled comparison vs matched real-NDE reports.

data_points.claim: But the signature NDE content is rare in DMT
data_points.detail: The honest disanalogy — quintessential in NDEs, least common in DMT.
About half of high-dose sessions involved being-contact.

data_points.claim: Early archive of internet trip reports mentioning entities
data_points.detail: Peter Meyer; argued the cross-report consistency makes them 'intersubjectively verifiable.'
